name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١)
```

```english
Whatever there is in the heavens and [whatever there is on] the earth glorifies Allah and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
```

```arabic
لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢)
```

```english
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: He gives life and brings death, and He has power over all things. (2)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلْأَوَّلُ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرُ وَٱلظَّـٰهِرُ وَٱلْبَاطِنُ ۖ وَهُوَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٣)
```

```english
He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He has knowledge of all things. (3)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا يَلِجُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا يَخْرُجُ مِنْهَا وَمَا يَنزِلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا يَعْرُجُ فِيهَا ۖ وَهُوَ مَعَكُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٤)
```

```english
It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days; then settled on the Throne. He knows whatever enters the earth and whatever emerges from it and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends to it, and He is with you wherever you may be, and Allah watches what you do. (4)
```

```arabic
لَّهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٥)
```

```english
To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned. (5)
```

```arabic
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٦)
```

```english
He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He knows best what is in the breasts. (6)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to urge the believers and incite them toward spending in the way of Allah (الإنفاق في سبيل الله), as indicated by the repeated emphasis on this command throughout its verses: **Believe in Allah and His Apostle, and spend out of that which He has made you successors** (57:7); **Who is it that will lend Allah a good loan** (57:11); and **Indeed the charitable men and charitable women, and those who lend Allah a good loan** (57:18). It has termed their spending as "lending" (إقراضا) to Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the best of those sought, and He does not fail His promise; He has promised them that if they lend to Him, He will multiply it for them and grant them a generous and abundant reward.

The Surah also points out that this spending is an act of godwariness (التقوى) — *the protective consciousness of Allah that shields one from His displeasure* — and faith in the Messenger, and that it entails the forgiveness of sins, the granting of two portions of mercy, the necessity of light, and even joining the ranks of the truthful (الصديقين) and the witnesses (الشهداء) before Allah, the Glorified.

Interspersed within its verses are items of knowledge pertaining to the Origin (المبدأ) — *the source of all existence, Allah* — and the Return (المعاد) — *the ultimate destination of all creation in the Hereafter* — as well as a call to godwariness (التقوى), sincerity of faith, asceticism (الزهد), and exhortation.

The Surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context of its verses, and some have claimed a consensus (إجماع) among exegetes on this matter.

The Surah opens by glorifying (التسبيح) and hallowing (التنزيه) Him, the Exalted, followed by several of His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى). This is because the purpose of the Surah—urging toward spending—might carry a faint suggestion of a perceived need or deficiency on His part. Thus, it is similar to all the Surahs that begin with glorification, namely the Surahs of *al-Hashr*, *al-Saff*, *al-Jumu'ah*, and *al-Taghabun*, which commence with "Glorifies" (سبح) or "Glorify" (يسبح).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise.** (57:1) Glorification (التسبيح) is hallowing (التنزيه), which is the negation of anything that implies deficiency or need, or anything that does not befit the presence of His perfection, the Exalted. The word "Whatever" (ما) is a relative pronoun referring to everything in the heavens and the earth, encompassing both rational beings (العقلاء) such as angels, humans, and jinn (الثقلين), and non-rational beings such as inanimate objects. The evidence for this is the subsequent mention of His attributes related to rational beings, such as giving life and knowledge of what is within the breasts.

The meaning is: everything in the heavens and the earth—which constitutes the entire universe—hallows Allah, the Glorified.

What is meant by their glorification is the literal meaning of glorification, not the metaphorical sense, which would be the mere indication of every existent's being in the heavens and the earth that it has a Creator hallowed from every deficiency and described by every perfection. Nor is it the general metaphor, which is the indication of every existent toward His hallowing, either by the tongue of speech (لسان القال) as with rational beings, or by the tongue of state (لسان الحال) as with non-rational existents. This is due to His saying, the Exalted: **and there is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification.** (17:44). Here, He makes an exception by stating that "you do not understand their glorification." If their glorification meant the indication of their existence toward His existence—which is the establishment of proof for people through their existence—or if it meant their glorification and praise by the tongue of state, these are things that people do understand, and thus the exception would have no meaning.

Therefore, the glorification of whatever is in the heavens and the earth is a true glorification and an utterance of hallowing in the literal sense of the word, even if we do not understand it. The Exalted said: **They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, He who gives speech to all things.’** (41:21).

And His saying: **and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise** (وهو العزيز الحكيم) means the One whose side is impregnable (المنيع), who overcomes and is never overcome; the One whose action is precise (المتقن), such that nothing occurs to His action that could spoil it for Him, nor does the objection of any objector attach to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth. He gives life and brings death, and He has power over all things.** (57:2) The discourse is structured to denote exclusivity (الحصر); thus, He alone is the Sovereign (المليك) in the heavens and the earth, decreeing whatever He wills because He is the Originator of everything. Whatever is in the heavens and the earth depends on Him for its existence and the effects of its existence; therefore, there is no decree except His, and no kingdom or authority except His.

His saying: **He gives life and brings death** (يحيي ويميت) is an allusion to the Names "The Giver of Life" (المحيي) and "The Bringer of Death" (المميت). The absolute usage of "gives life and brings death" implies their encompassment of every instance of giving life and bringing death, such as His creating the angels alive without prior death, His giving life to the fetus in its mother's womb, His giving life to the dead in the Resurrection, His creating inanimate matter dead without prior life, His bringing death to man in this world, and His bringing death a second time in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — as indicated by His saying: **‘Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life.’** (40:11). The form "gives life and brings death" (يحيي ويميت) indicates continuity (الاستمرار).

His saying: **and He has power over all things** (وهو على كل شيء قدير) contains an allusion to the attribute of His power (القدرة) and that it is absolute, not restricted to one thing over another. The conclusion of the verse with the mention of power over all things is appropriate to the preceding mention of giving life and bringing death, as someone might imagine that there is no power to revive the dead once no trace or essence of them remains.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **He is the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden, and He has knowledge of all things.** (57:3) Since He, the Exalted, is powerful over every conceivable thing, He encompasses everything with His power from every direction. Thus, whatever is assumed to be "first," He is before it; so He is the First (الأول) rather than the thing assumed to be first. Whatever is assumed to be "last," He is after it due to His power encompassing it from every side; so He is the Last (الآخر) rather than the thing assumed to be last. Whatever thing is assumed to be "manifest," He is more manifest than it because His power encompasses it from above it; so He is the Manifest (الظاهر) rather than the thing assumed to be manifest. And whatever thing is assumed to be "hidden," He, the Exalted, is more hidden than it because of His encompassment of it from behind it; so He is the Hidden (الباطن) rather than the thing assumed to be hidden. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden in an absolute sense, while these attributes in anything other than Him are relative and proportional.

His being the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden is neither temporal (زمانية) nor spatial (مكانية) in the sense of Him being contained by them; otherwise, He would not precede them nor be hallowed from them. Rather, He encompasses all things in whatever manner they are assumed and however they are conceived.

It becomes clear from the preceding that these four names—the First, the Last, the Manifest, and the Hidden—are branches of His name "The Encompassing" (المحيط), which is itself a branch of the absoluteness of His power (القدرة); for His power encompasses everything. It is also possible to derive these four names from the encompassment of His existence (الوجود) over everything; for He, the Exalted, is established before the establishment of everything, and established after the annihilation of everything, and is closer than every manifest thing, and more hidden from delusions and intellects than every concealed, hidden thing.

Similarly, these four names have a type of derivation from His knowledge (العلم), which is suited by the conclusion of the verse with His saying: **and He has knowledge of all things** (وهو بكل شيء عليم).

Some have interpreted the four names as: He is the First before everything, the Last after the destruction of everything, the Manifest through the proofs indicating Him, and the Hidden who is not perceived by the senses.

It has also been said: The First without a beginning, the Last without an end, the Manifest who is dominant and exalted over everything such that everything is beneath Him, and the Hidden who knows everything such that no one is more knowledgeable than Him.

It has also been said: The First without beginning, the Last without end, the Manifest without proximity, and the Hidden without concealment.

There are other opinions regarding their meanings that are not sound, which we have refrained from mentioning.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **It is He who created the heavens and the earth in six days** (57:4). Its explanation has preceded.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **then He settled on the Throne. He knows whatever travels into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it.** (57:4). A detailed discussion on the meaning of the Throne (العرش) preceded in Surah *al-A'raf*, verse 54.

It was previously mentioned that "settling on the Throne" is a metaphor for taking up the governance of the kingdom (تدبير الملك); therefore, He followed it with the mention of knowledge of particular states, because knowledge is a necessary concomitant of governance.

His saying: **He knows whatever travels into the earth and whatever comes out of it, and whatever descends from the sky and whatever ascends into it.** Traveling into (الولوج)—as al-Raghib said—is entering into a narrow space, and ascending (العروج) is going upward. The meaning is: He knows what enters and penetrates the earth, such as rainwater, seeds, and other things; what comes out of the earth, such as types of plants, animals, and water; what descends from the sky, such as rain, rays, and angels; and what ascends and goes up into it, such as vapors, angels, and the deeds of the servants.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **and He is with you wherever you may be** (57:4) because of His encompassment of you; thus, you are never absent from Him wherever you are, in whatever time you live, or in whatever state you are assumed to be. He mentioned the generality of places—"wherever you may be"—because the most familiar way for one thing to separate from another and be absent from it is by changing location; otherwise, His relation to all places, times, and states is the same.

It has been said: "Withness" (المعية) is a metaphor for His encompassment in knowledge (الإحاطة العلمية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **and Allah sees best what you do.** (57:4). This is like a branch resulting from what preceded it—His being with them wherever they are and His being knowledgeable of all things. For the necessary consequence of His presence with them without any separation or concealment, while He is All-knowing, is that He is All-seeing (بصيرا) of their actions, seeing the outward aspect of their deeds and what is in their inward selves of intention and purpose.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **To Him belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah all matters are returned.** (57:5). He repeated His saying: "To Him belongs the kingdom..." etc., because the return of all things to Him is built upon the generality of His kingdom; thus, He stated it explicitly to indicate this foundation. The Exalted said: **The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah. ‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’** (40:16).

His saying: **and to Allah all matters are returned** (وإلى الله ترجع الأمور). "Matters" (الأمور) is a plural defined by the definite article, which denotes generality, as in His saying: **Look! To Allah do all matters eventually come.** (42:53). Thus, there is nothing but that it returns to Allah, and there is no one to return them to Allah except Him, because the kingdom belongs exclusively to Him; so to Him belongs the command and to Him belongs the judgment.

In the verse, the manifest noun "Allah" is placed where a pronoun would be in "to Allah" (إلى الله), and likewise in the previous verse "and Allah sees best what you do." Perhaps the reason for this is so that the two sentences may strike their hearts like a well-known proverb, because of the upcoming mention of the Day of Resurrection and the magnificent reward for those who spend in the way of Allah therein.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He knows best what is in the breasts.** (57:6). Making the night pass into the day and the day into the night refers to the variation of the night and day in length and shortness according to the seasons of the year in each of the northern and southern regions, each being the opposite of the other. This has been mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, more than once.

What is meant by "what is in the breasts" (ذات الصدور) are the concealed thoughts and hidden intentions that accompany the breasts, because they are attributed to the hearts, and the hearts are in the breasts. The sentence—meaning His saying: **and He knows best what is in the breasts**—is an explanation of His knowledge encompassing what is in the breasts, after explaining His sight encompassing the outward aspects of their deeds with His saying: **and Allah sees best what you do.**

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, it is narrated by Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi (who graded it as fair), al-Nasa'i, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman*, from 'Irbad ibn Sariyah: that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to recite the *Musabbihat* (the Surahs beginning with glorification) before he went to sleep, and he said: "In them is a verse better than a thousand verses."

**I say:** It is also narrated from Ibn al-Durays from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from 'Asim ibn Humayd, he said: Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام was asked about monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and he said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, knew that in the end of times there would be people who delve deeply [into matters], so Allah the Exalted revealed: **Say, ‘He is Allah, the One’** (Surah 112) and the verses from Surah *al-Hadid* up to His saying: **and He knows best what is in the breasts.** So whoever seeks beyond that has perished."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding **Whatever is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise**, he said: It is His saying: "I have been given the comprehensive words (جوامع الكلم)." And regarding His saying: **He is the First**, he said: "Meaning before everything." **and the Last**, he said: "He remains after everything." **and He knows best what is in the breasts**, he said: "Of the secret thoughts (الضمائر)."

In *al-Kafi*, it is narrated: that he—meaning Ali عليه‌السلام—was asked: "Where was our Lord before He created the heaven and the earth?" He replied: "'Where' is a question about place (مكان), and Allah was while there was no place."

In *al-Tawhid*, a sermon by al-Hasan ibn Ali عليه‌السلام contains: "Praise be to Allah, for whom there was no known first (أول معلوم), nor a finite last (آخر متناه), nor a perceivable 'before' (قبل مدرك), nor a limited 'after' (بعد محدود). Thus, intellects and their delusions, and thought and its passing reflections, and minds and their perceptions do not grasp His attribute, such that one could say: 'When?' or 'Begun from what?' or 'Manifest upon what?' or 'Hidden within what?'"

**I say:** His saying "known first" etc., are explanatory descriptions; meaning He has no first, for if He had a first, it would be possible for knowledge to attach to it. Nor a last, for if He had a last, He would be finite. Nor a "before," for if there were, it would be possible to perceive it. Nor an "after," otherwise He would be limited.

His saying: "Begun from what?" means He did not begin from anything such that He would have a first. "Manifest upon what?" means He does not excel over something by occurring and settling upon it, like a body upon a body. "Hidden within what?" means He is not hidden within something by entering it and being concealed by it.

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: "Every manifest thing other than Him is not hidden, and every hidden thing other than Him is not manifest."

**I say:** Its meaning is that the aspect of manifestation (الظهور) in anything other than Him, the Exalted, is different from the aspect of hiddenness (البطون), and vice versa. As for Him, the Exalted, since He is unitary in essence (أحدي الذات), neither dividing nor partitioning into one side and another, He is manifest from the very aspect He is hidden, and hidden from the very aspect He is manifest. Thus, He is a hidden one, concealed due to the perfection of His manifestation, and a manifest one, clear due to the perfection of His hiddenness.

Also in it: "Praise be to Allah, the First, so there is nothing before Him; and the Last, so there is nothing after Him; and the Manifest, so there is nothing above Him; and the Hidden, so there is nothing beneath Him."

**I say:** What is meant by priority (القبلية) and posteriority (البعدية) is not temporal priority and posteriority, such that one would assume a temporal extension without ends, in which the world occupies a portion while its two ends are empty of it, and the existence of Allah, the Hallowed and Exalted, would correspond to the entirety of time, with nothing of its two sides being empty of Him even if they went to infinity—such that His existence, the Exalted, would precede the world in time and follow it in time. If it were so, He, the Exalted, would be subject to change in His essence and states by the change of times renewing upon Him, and His priority and posteriority would be subordinate to time, and time would be the "First" and the "Last" essentially.

Likewise, His manifestation and hiddenness are not according to place (المكان) in a similar vein. Rather, He, the Exalted, precedes by His very transcendent essence every assumed thing, and is the last by His very essence after every matter assumed to be last; and He is manifest and hidden likewise. Time is His creation, posterior to Him.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu al-Shaykh narrates in *al-'Azamah* from Ibn Umar and Abu Sa'id from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "People will not cease asking about everything until they say: 'This is Allah, He was before everything, so what was before Allah?' If they say that to you, then say: 'He is the First before everything, and He is the Last so there is nothing after Him, and He is the Manifest above everything, and He is the Hidden beneath everything, and He has knowledge of all things.'"

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain to Abu Basir, he said: I heard Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: "Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, our Lord, has always been, and knowledge (العلم) was His essence while there was nothing to be known (معلوم). Then, when He brought things into existence, the knowledge from Him fell upon the known."

**I say:** What is meant by this knowledge is not mental images (الصور الذهنية), such that He, the Exalted, would be like the builder of a house who conceives a form and shape for the house before building it, then builds it according to what he conceived, so the mental image corresponds to the external structure, and then the house is demolished while the mental image remains as it was. This is what is called "universal knowledge" (العلم الكلي), and it is impossible for Him, the Exalted. Rather, His essence, the Exalted, is identical to the knowledge of His object of knowledge; then, when the object of knowledge is realized in the external world, the essence of the object of knowledge is identical to His knowledge of it. The first is called "Essential Knowledge" (العلم الذاتي) and the second "Active Knowledge" (العلم الفعلي).

Also in it, a sermon by Ali عليه‌السلام contains: "And He knew them not by an instrument (أداة) without which knowledge cannot be; and there is not between Him and His object of knowledge any knowledge other than Him."

**I say:** What is meant by this is that His essence, the Exalted, is identical to His knowledge, and there is no additional form (صورة زائدة).
